title: Unit Final: Self-Assessment and Career Exploration

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of self-assessment in career exploration?

choices.text: To identify personal traits that may connect to career options
choices.feedback: Correct. Self-assessment helps students identify interests, strengths, values, and preferences that may connect to careers.

choices.text: To choose one job immediately without more learning
choices.feedback: Self-assessment supports exploration, but it does not replace further learning.

choices.text: To focus only on salary and ignore personal traits
choices.feedback: Career exploration should include personal traits, not salary alone.

choices.text: To avoid comparing different career pathways
choices.feedback: Students still need to compare pathways and career options.

questions.prompt: Values can affect whether a career feels like a good fit for a student.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Values do affect career fit because they shape decisions about priorities and goals.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Values help students decide what matters most in future work and life choices.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a career pathway?

choices.text: A single class required for graduation
choices.feedback: A class may support planning, but it is not a career pathway.

choices.text: A school activity chosen for one semester
choices.feedback: An activity is not the same as a career pathway.

choices.text: A more specific route within a broad career cluster
choices.feedback: Correct. A pathway is a narrower route inside a larger career cluster.

choices.text: A list of random job titles with no connection
choices.feedback: Career pathways group related options, not random job titles.

questions.prompt: A student enjoys helping others, communicates clearly, and wants a people-focused work environment. What is the best next step in career exploration?

choices.text: Ignore those traits and choose a career at random
choices.feedback: Career exploration should use personal evidence, not random choice.

choices.text: Decide that only one career is possible without more research
choices.feedback: Students should still compare more than one option.

choices.text: Focus only on which job seems most popular
choices.feedback: Popularity is not the best basis for career exploration.

choices.text: Use those traits to explore career clusters and options that may fit
choices.feedback: Correct. Personal traits should guide students toward clusters and careers that may be a stronger fit.

questions.prompt: Work preferences can include schedule, environment, and whether someone likes working alone or with others.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Work preferences often include schedule, environment, pace, and the way a person likes to work.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Those are all common examples of work preferences.

questions.prompt: Which option best shows the difference between an interest and a strength?

choices.text: An interest is what matters most in life, and a strength is a career cluster
choices.feedback: This mixes up values, strengths, and career clusters.

choices.text: An interest is something a person enjoys, and a strength is something a person does well
choices.feedback: Correct. Interests describe enjoyment, while strengths describe abilities or skills.

choices.text: An interest is a school rule, and a strength is a hobby
choices.feedback: Interests and strengths are personal traits, not school rules.

choices.text: An interest is a final job choice, and a strength is a schedule preference
choices.feedback: This does not correctly define either term.

questions.prompt: A student values stability, prefers structured tasks, and likes organizing information. Which conclusion is most reasonable?

choices.text: The student should ignore those traits and choose any pathway
choices.feedback: Those traits should be considered during career matching.

choices.text: The student has already chosen a final career with no need to compare options
choices.feedback: Students should still compare different options.

choices.text: Those traits matter only after applying for a job
choices.feedback: These traits matter early in career exploration.

choices.text: Those traits may help the student identify career options that match those preferences
choices.feedback: Correct. Values and preferences can help students narrow careers that fit them better.

questions.prompt: Why is it helpful to learn about career clusters before choosing a specific career?

choices.text: Because career clusters help students explore related options instead of only one job
choices.feedback: This idea is correct, but the best answer here is the one that emphasizes organized exploration of related industries.

choices.text: Because career clusters replace the need for self-reflection
choices.feedback: Students still need self-reflection along with career exploration.

choices.text: Because career clusters organize related jobs and industries into clearer categories
choices.feedback: Correct. Career clusters help students understand related categories and compare connected career options.

choices.text: Because career clusters guarantee the highest-paying career
choices.feedback: Career clusters organize options, but they do not guarantee salary outcomes.

questions.prompt: Matching self to careers can involve reflection and change as a student learns more.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Career matching can change over time as students gain more self-awareness and career knowledge.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Career matching is a process that can develop and change.

questions.prompt: A student is interested in design, enjoys solving creative problems, values flexible work, and prefers project-based tasks. What does this information best support?

choices.text: A career match based on personal interests, values, and preferences
choices.feedback: This is partly correct, but the best answer is the most direct statement about using multiple personal traits in career matching.

choices.text: The idea that personal evidence can help identify careers that may fit well
choices.feedback: Correct. Personal evidence can help students identify career options that may fit well.

choices.text: The idea that one school subject should decide every future choice
choices.feedback: Career matching should use several factors, not one school subject.

choices.text: The process of using several personal traits to explore likely career fits
choices.feedback: Using several traits is important, but this choice is broader than the strongest answer about how personal evidence supports career fit.
